Name: compression_theory
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of neural network compression can be understood through the lens of rate-distortion theory, which provides a mathematical framework for balancing the trade-off between compression rate and distortion. By applying this framework to neural networks, we can derive achievable bounds on the compression rate and identify the most compressible components of the network.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic techniques for neural network compression and pruning. However, these works have primarily focused on heuristic methods and empirical evaluations, rather than providing a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding the fundamental limits of compression. Our proposal builds upon this existing work by providing a theoretical foundation for neural network compression, grounded in the principles of rate-distortion theory.
Abstract: Neural network compression is a crucial technique for reducing the computational requirements and memory footprint of deep learning models. However, the fundamental limits of compression are not well understood, and existing methods often rely on heuristic techniques and empirical evaluations. In this proposal, we apply the principles of rate-distortion theory to derive achievable bounds on the compression rate of neural networks. We demonstrate that this framework provides a powerful tool for understanding the trade-off between compression rate and distortion, and for identifying the most compressible components of the network. Our approach has the potential to provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for neural network compression, and to inspire the development of new compression algorithms and techniques.
Experiments: We will conduct a series of experiments to validate our proposal and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. These experiments will include: (1) deriving achievable bounds on the compression rate of neural networks using rate-distortion theory; (2) evaluating the performance of our compression algorithm on a range of benchmark datasets and models; and (3) comparing our approach to existing heuristic methods for neural network compression and pruning. We will use metrics such as compression rate, distortion, and computational requirements to evaluate the performance of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor for our proposal is the complexity of the mathematical framework, which may require significant expertise in rate-distortion theory and information theory. Additionally, our approach may be limited by the availability of computational resources and the scalability of our algorithms. However, we believe that the potential benefits of our approach, including the provision of a rigorous mathematical foundation for neural network compression and the inspiration of new compression algorithms and techniques, outweigh these risks.
